title: The Serpent Power: The Cakra and Kundalini Doctrine of Arthur Avalon
subtitle: Sir John Woodroffe's (Arthur Avalon) Exposition of the Sat-Cakra-Nirupana and the Paduka-Pancaka — the Six Cakras, Kundalini, and the Ascent to Sahasrara

summary.one_line: Arthur Avalon's (Sir John Woodroffe) The Serpent Power — the definitive translation and commentary on the Sat-Cakra-Nirupana and Paduka-Pancaka, the foundational texts of Kuṇḍalinī Yoga and the six...
summary.scope: Covers the full text of The Serpent Power (1919), including the translation of the Sat-Cakra-Nirupana (Śrī Pūrṇānanda's description of the six cakras and the kuṇḍalinī), the Paduka-Pañcaka (the fivefold footstool), and Woodroffe's extensive commentary on the nāḍī system, bija mantras, and the ascent to sahasrāra.

sources.label: The Serpent Power: The Secrets of Tantric and Shaktic Yoga — Arthur Avalon (Sir John Woodroffe, 1919)

body.text: [Sat-Cakra-Nirupana, vv. 1–11; Woodroffe commentary] The Kuṇḍalinī is described as the coiled power (kuṇḍala = coil), residing in the mūlādhāra cakra between the anus and the genitals. She is the Śakti who sleeps, coiled three and a half times around the Svayaṃbhu Liṅga, closing the mouth of the Brahmadvāra with her head. She is the 'serpent power,' the primordial cosmic energy that lies latent in every human being. Woodroffe explains that Kuṇḍalinī is no mere physical force but the 'supreme Śakti' in the body — the same power that creates, sustains, and dissolves the universe, now manifesting as the individual's own vital spiritual energy. She is described as 'the light of the Liṅga' and as 'more subtle than the lotus fibre.' When awakened, she straightens, enters the suṣumnā nāḍī, and begins her ascent through the cakras.

[Sat-Cakra-Nirupana, vv. 1–38; Woodroffe commentary] The six cakras are the centers of consciousness (padma, lotus) arranged along the spinal column: (1) Mūlādhāra (root support) at the perineum, four red petals, with the seed mantra LAṄ, the earth element, and the Indra's region; (2) Svādhiṣṭhāna (self-station) at the genitals, six vermilion petals, seed mantra VAṄ, water element, and Varuṇa's region; (3) Maṇipūra (jewel city) at the navel, ten gray petals, seed mantra RAṄ, fire element, and the sun's region; (4) Anāhata (unstruck sound) at the heart, twelve red petals, seed mantra YAṄ, air element, and the moon's region; (5) Viśuddha (pure) at the throat, sixteen purple petals, seed mantra HAṄ, ether element, and the Akāśa region; (6) Ājñā (command) between the eyebrows, two white petals, seed mantra KṢAṄ, the mind element, and the region of the Supreme Guru. Above these is the thousand-petalled Sahasrāra at the crown.

[Woodroffe commentary on the nāḍīs] The nāḍīs are the subtle nerve-channels (not physical nerves) through which prāṇa flows. The Ṣaṭ-Cakra-Nirūpaṇa and its commentaries enumerate 72,000 nāḍīs, but only fourteen are of primary importance. The three principal nāḍīs are: (1) Suṣumnā, the central channel running within the spinal column from the mūlādhāra to the brahmarandhra (aperture at the crown); (2) Iḍā, the left channel, carrying the lunar or cooling current, associated with the jīva and the left nostril; (3) Piṅgalā, the right channel, carrying the solar or heating current, associated with vitality and the right nostril. The Kuṇḍalinī, when awakened, enters the suṣumnā, burning through the three knots (granthis): Brahmā-granthi at the mūlādhāra, Viṣṇu-granthi at the anāhata, and Rudra-granthi at the ājñā.

[Sat-Cakra-Nirupana, vv. 1–38; Paduka-Pancaka, vv. 1–5; Woodroffe commentary] Each cakra has its own seed mantra (bīja), deity, and element. The six seed mantras are: LAṄ (earth, Mūlādhāra), VAṄ (water, Svādhiṣṭhāna), RAṄ (fire, Maṇipūra), YAṄ (air, Anāhata), HAṄ (ether, Viśuddha), and KṢAṄ (mind, Ājñā). The presiding deities are: Brahmā, Viṣṇu, and Rudra in the lower cakras; Īśa and Sadāśiva in the upper. The goddesses are the Śaktis: Dākinī, Śākinī, Kākinī, Lākinī, and Śākinī again in the viśuddha. The Paduka-Pancaka adds: the bīja of the kuṇḍalinī is the Haṃsa-mantra, and the Haṃsa (swan) symbolizes the individual jīvātman. Woodroffe emphasizes that these mantras are not merely symbolic but are actual sound-vibrations that correspond to the tattvas (elements) being realized.

[Woodroffe introduction, Sat-Cakra-Nirupana complete] The Ṣaṭ-Cakra-Nirūpaṇa ('Description of and Investigation into the Six Cakras') is the foundational Sanskrit text translated and commented upon in The Serpent Power. It was composed by Śrī Pūrṇānanda Svāmī (c. 1577 CE), a Bengali Śākta Yogī of the Varendra clan, as part of his larger work, the Śrītattva-cintāmaṇi. The text contains 56 verses organized in six chapters, each describing one cakra from mūlādhāra to ājñā, followed by the sahasrāra. Woodroffe's edition includes the Sanskrit text in Devanāgarī, roman transliteration, translation, and extensive commentary drawing from the Ṣaṭ-cakra-bheda and other tantric commentaries. The text is remarkable for its systematic description of each cakra's petal-number, color, bīja, deity, element, and the corresponding states of consciousness.

[Paduka-Pancaka, vv. 1–10; Woodroffe commentary] The Pādukā-Pañcaka ('Fivefold Footstool') is the second text translated in The Serpent Power. It consists of five verses describing the five stages or stations of the yogic path: (1) the yoga-pīṭha (the seat of union), (2) the pādukā (the feet of the guru — the śaktipāta or descent of grace), (3) the liṅgam (the supreme sign), (4) the praṇava (the OM mantra), and (5) the śabda-brahma (sound as the Absolute). Woodroffe interprets the five as a progressive revelation of the nature of reality: from the concrete support of practice, through the guru's grace, to the realization of the Supreme as vibration (spanda). The Paduka-Pancaka thus serves as a condensed theological summary of the entire kuṇḍalinī path.

[Sat-Cakra-Nirupana, vv. 39–49; Woodroffe commentary] The sahasrāra (the thousand-petalled lotus) is above all six cakras, located at the crown of the head, in the brahmarandhra. It is not itself a cakra but the supreme abode of Śiva-Śakti united. When the kuṇḍalinī pierces the ājñā cakra, she passes through to the sahasrāra, where she unites with Śiva. The description: 'Within the lotus of the thousand petals, in the midst of the full moon, the supreme blissful Kuṇḍalinī, together with Śiva, is seen.' The union of Śiva and Śakti in the sahasrāra is the goal — the state of absolute consciousness (cit) and bliss (ānanda). The aspirant who attains this is liberated while living (jīvan-mukta). Woodroffe emphasizes that this is not a mere physiological event but a transformation of consciousness, the full awakening of the spiritual self.

[Woodroffe commentary, Part II: The Practice] The awakening of the kuṇḍalinī is accomplished through a combination of means: (1) Ṣaṭ-karma (the six purificatory actions of haṭha yoga), (2) prāṇāyāma (breath control) to force the suṣumnā, (3) mantropāsanā (worship through mantra), specifically the bija mantras of the cakras, (4) dhyāna (meditation) on the form of the kuṇḍalinī and the cakras, (5) guru-kṛpā (the grace of the guru) as the indispensable catalyst, and (6) the cultivation of bhakti (devotion) and sattvic conduct. Woodroffe warns that the awakening can be dangerous without proper guidance — the untrained aspirant risks madness, disease, or death if the kuṇḍalinī is forced prematurely. The safest method is the 'natural path' of purification, mantra japa, and devotion under a qualified guru, allowing the awakening to occur spontaneously through grace.

gaps: Additional passages needed on the dhyāna descriptions of each cakra, the tattva-viveka (discrimination of elements), and the relationship between the cakras and the senses
The specific prāṇāyāma sequences, āsanas, mudrās, and bandhas mentioned in Woodroffe's commentary need full extraction

issues.title: Passages are summary extracts, not verbatim text
issues.description: Current passages summarize content from Woodroffe's translation and commentary. Full verbatim extracts with verse references need to be added from the source text.

issues.title: Source file may contain incorrect content
issues.description: The file at /root/projects/blog/content/sources/occult/fundamental/texts/serpent-power.txt (3546 lines) appears to contain the text of 'Timid Lucy' by Sarah S. Baker rather than 'The Serpent Power.' The actual Serpent Power text needs to be located or re-downloaded.

issues.title: Missing comparative texts on the cakra system
issues.description: No secondary sources on the cakra system from other traditions (e.g., Buddhist, Jain, Theosophical interpretations) have been included for comparison.

versions.summary: Initial RO created based on the user's content knowledge of The Serpent Power. Eight passages cover: Kundalini as coiled power, the six cakras, the nāḍī system, the bija mantras, the Sat-Cakra-Nirupana, the Paduka-Pancaka, the ascent to sahasrāra, and the method of awakening.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state. Source file at serpent-power.txt contains incorrect content (Timid Lucy instead of The Serpent Power). Actual text needs to be located.
